retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Peritoneum -- Clinical Significance -- Ascites
retrieved_docs.content: Peritoneal Dialysis  Infusion of a hypertonic fluid in the peritoneal cavity performs peritoneal dialysis. Once the waste products are absorbed, the fluid is then drained out. Several cycles are performed each time. The most common complication of peritoneal dialysis is the risk of infection. Another complication that is not rare is a perforation of bowel when the dialysis catheter is inserted inside the peritoneal cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Surface Malignancies -- Pathophysiology -- Peritoneal Cavity Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The constant movement of peritoneal fluid, driven partly by diaphragmatic contractions and gravity, influences where malignant cells may implant within the abdomen (see Image . Pathways of Peritoneal Fluid Movement). This explains why certain areas, including the subphrenic spaces due to diaphragmatic "pull" forces, paracolic gutters, and the pelvis, which is gravity-dependent, are more commonly involved in peritoneal disease. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum is the serous membrane that lines the abdominal cavity, supporting the abdominal viscera and facilitating the passage of blood, lymph, and nerve impulses. The peritoneum consists of 2 layers: the parietal peritoneum lining the abdominal wall and the visceral peritoneum surrounding the organs. The space between these 2 layers is known as the abdominal cavity or coelom. Within this space lies the peritoneal fluid, which envelops abdominal organs and aids in lubricating peristaltic movements. The volume of peritoneal fluid typically ranges around 100 mL. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pleural Fluid -- Organ Systems Involved
retrieved_docs.content: The pleural fluid is contained in the pleural cavity, which is the space between the internal thoracic wall and the lungs. The pleural cavity is lined by a fibrous mesothelial membrane that is made up of a parietal and visceral layer. The parietal layer is the lining of the internal thoracic cavity, and the visceral layer covers the outside of the lungs. These layers are continuous and meet to form a double layer at the hilum of each lung, with no communication between the right and left pleural cavities.

retrieved_docs.title: Encapsulating Peritoneal Sclerosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis is multifactorial and not fully understood. Long-term peritoneal dialysis is a significant risk factor, and patients that require treatment will experience adverse cellular changes to the peritoneal membrane due to exposure to bioincompatible peritoneal dialysis fluids. Approximately 50 to 80% of all patients on peritoneal dialysis will experience progressive sclerosis of the peritoneal membrane within one to two years of initiating treatment. The chemical composition of the solutions used in peritoneal dialysis causes oxidative stress, which damages the peritoneal membrane. The acidic pH, elevated osmolarity, and increased glucose concentration cause fibrosis, vasculopathy, and loss of peritoneal ultrafiltration. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Peritoneum -- Clinical Significance -- Ascites
retrieved_docs.content: Normally, the peritoneal space only contains up to 100 mL of serous fluid. In various situations, such as cirrhosis or chyle leaks, there can be a pathologic increase in peritoneal fluid volume. Cirrhotic ascites is believed to be due to portal hypertension, leading to increased permeability in blood vessels, allowing for altered oncotic and hydrostatic pressures that result in an imbalance of protein and electrolytes, thus altering the fluid flow. [13] In chylous ascites, there is an increase in lymphatic fluid in the peritoneal cavity, which can be secondary to a chyle leak or can occur from surgery or trauma. [14] Treatment is primarily medical but can be surgical on occasion.

retrieved_docs.title: Paracentesis -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Paracentesis is a procedure performed in patients with ascites, in which a needle is inserted into the peritoneal cavity to obtain or remove ascitic fluid. Ascites, defined as the abnormal fluid accumulation within the peritoneal cavity, typically arise from portal hypertension, decreased oncotic pressure, or impaired lymphatic drainage. In cirrhosis, increased hydrostatic pressure within the splanchnic circulation and hypoalbuminemia result in progressive fluid accumulation. Removing this fluid through paracentesis alleviates intra-abdominal pressure, restores diaphragmatic excursion, and improves symptoms such as abdominal discomfort, dyspnea, and early satiety. However, large-volume fluid removal can precipitate paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction, underscoring the importance of intravenous (IV) albumin infusion when more than 5 liters are drained.

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Surface Malignancies -- Pathophysiology -- Peritoneal Surface Malignancy Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: PSMs primarily spread through transcoelomic dissemination, a process in which tumor cells detach from the primary tumor and migrate within the peritoneal cavity. Tumor cells actively breach the basement membrane and subsequently adhere to and infiltrate the mesothelial lining of the peritoneum. As the peritoneal cavity is a large, serous surface with constantly moving peritoneal fluid, malignant cells spread throughout the cavity, evading immune cells and utilizing local growth factors to multiply.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pleural Fluid -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An increase of both capillary and mesothelial water permeability leads to hypooncotic fluid (lower protein content), and if filtration exceeds the maximum lymph reabsorption through the parietal stomata, transudate forms. Transudative pleural effusions occur in congestive heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome and malnutrition. The last three conditions reflect a decrease in colloid oncotic pressure due to hypoalbuminemia. Localized pleural fluid effusion seen from a pulmonary embolism may result from increased capillary permeability due to cytokine and inflammatory mediator release from the platelet-rich thrombi.

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Surface Malignancies -- Pathophysiology -- Peritoneal Surface Malignancy Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, the peritoneal cavity itself actively participates in the metastatic process. The peritoneal fluid circulates continuously within the cavity, disseminating tumor cells throughout the abdomen. The mesothelial cells lining the peritoneum release cytokines and growth factors in response to the presence of tumor cells, which may inadvertently create a supportive microenvironment that promotes the survival and proliferation of tumor cells. [6] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dialysis Catheter -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: By contrast, peritoneal dialysis catheters target the intraperitoneal space rather than a vascular structure. When correctly placed, they should traverse the anterior abdominal wall with the distal tip resting superficial to the visceral peritoneum and deep to the parietal peritoneum, pointed in the direction of the pelvis. [4] The borders of the rectus muscle are preferred insertion sites, away from the paths of the superficial and inferior epigastric arteries. With the use of a Tenckhoff catheter with two cuffs, the deeper cuff should rest within the pre-peritoneal space in the rectus sheath, and the superficial cuff should lie 2 to 3cm medial to the superficial wound. [4] Placing the deeper cuff outside the rectus muscle may lead to less tissue ingrowth, increasing the likelihood of leakage and herniation. If the superficial cuff placement is too deep, serous fluid may collect in the space outside of the cuff, leading to skin irritation and infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Dialysis -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The process of PD can be automated using a simple machine attached to peritoneal dialysis fluid bags. The machine is programmed to infuse the predetermined volume of fluid into the peritoneal cavity, dwell fluid in the peritoneal cavity for a specified time, and automatically drain the fluid. Cycles are repeated several times based on patients needs and peritoneal membrane diffusion characteristics. Manual exchanges require the installation and draining of fluid to be performed by the patient, repeating the process several times during the day to achieve the required clearance of toxins.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Lymphatic Drainage -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple factors can obstruct lymph flow. One factor depends on how hydrated the interstitial compartment is. Fluid crosses through the interstitial space across cells to eventually reach the lymphatic vessels. In instances of inflammation, filtration through the interstitium increases, which allows for the quick transfer of proteins and other molecules to the lymphatic system. When there is a decrease in the flow or dehydration of fluid across the interstitial space, it backs up into the extracellular space leading to swelling or lymphedema.  Others include external forces such as impairment in gut motility, causing impingement on the flow and internal forces where the lymphatic system has a deficit in contractility. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Peritoneum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum is the serous membrane that lines the abdominal cavity. It is composed of mesothelial cells that are supported by a thin layer of fibrous tissue and is embryologically derived from the mesoderm. The peritoneum serves to support the organs of the abdomen and acts as a conduit for the passage of nerves, blood vessels, and lymphatics. Although the peritoneum is thin, it is made of 2 layers with a potential space between them. The potential space between the 2 layers contains about 50 to 100 ml of serous fluid that prevents friction and allows the layers and organs to glide freely. [1] The outer layer is the parietal peritoneum, which attaches to the abdominal and pelvic walls. The inner visceral layer wraps around the internal organs located inside the intraperitoneal space. The structures bound by the peritoneal cavity may be intraperitoneal or retroperitoneal.

retrieved_docs.title: Pleural Effusion -- Introduction -- Pleural Space Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The visceral and parietal pleurae enclose the pleural cavity, a potential space typically containing only a minimum volume of pleural fluid. This fluid minimizes friction between the pleural layers during respiratory movements. The volumetric fluid balance within the pleural space is determined by gravity, ventilatory motion, and hydrostatic and oncotic pressures. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Compartment Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical procedures that carry an especially high risk of developing IAH include liver transplantation, damage control surgery, repair of abdominal aortic aneurysm, and large abdominal hernia repairs. [10] Other etiologies must be considered in addition to surgery as potential causes of ACS, which can be classified as primary or secondary. Primary ACS causes include blunt or penetrating trauma, intra-abdominal hemorrhage, abdominal aortic aneurysm rupture, abdominal adhesions, intestinal obstruction, tumor formation, and retroperitoneal hematoma. Secondary causes do not originate from intra-abdominal disease, including pregnancy, ascites, ileus, intra-abdominal sepsis, and large-volume fluid resuscitation. [11] Intra-abdominal pressures can be chronically elevated in pregnancy, cirrhosis, obesity, intra-abdominal malignancy, and peritoneal dialysis. [6] [12] [13] [14] Those with higher body mass index can be expected to have a higher baseline IAP of 9 to 14 mm Hg due to the restrictive effect of adipose tissue. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal paracentesis is a medical procedure that is directly associated with الاسْتِسْقَاء (ascites). It is performed to obtain peritoneal fluid for diagnostic evaluation or to therapeutically drain excess fluid in patients who have ascites, thereby assisting clinicians in determining the underlying cause of the fluid accumulation and providing symptom relief.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 942
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal paracentesis is a medical procedure that is broadly related to the abdomen, referred to as břicho. The anatomical region břicho includes clinical concepts such as بَطْنٌ حادّ (acute abdomen) and may present with findings like كتلة في البَطْن (abdominal mass). The purpose of abdominal paracentesis involves الاسْتِسْقَاء, which denotes the aspiration of fluid from the peritoneal cavity, and it is used to diagnose and manage conditions affecting břicho, including acute abdominal presentations and abdominal masses.

Fallopian tube surgeries (العمليات الجراحية لقناة فالوب) are considered a specific subset of abdominal surgeries. Overall, these relationships illustrate how abdominal destructive procedures, renal endoscopic interventions, and peritoneal surgeries are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (abdomen, kidneys, ureter, peritoneum), procedural classifications, and associated clinical findings, emphasizing the integrated nature of urologic and abdominal surgical care.

Finally, procedures involving the retroperitoneal space demonstrate site‑specific relationships: removal of a tube from the retroperitoneum is an indirect therapeutic procedure, while diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum is a direct diagnostic procedure targeting the same compartment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 925
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrotoxic acute renal failure is recognized within the FDA Structured Product Labeling Terminology as a Substance that functions as a causative_agent_of renal injury. The FDA Structured Product Labeling Terminology itself includes the broader concept of Substance, which is directly_substance_of a variety of medical procedures. Substance is directly linked to abdominal paracentesis, a procedure used to drain fluid from the peritoneal cavity, and is also associated with categories such as injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints and soft tissue; other non‑therapeutic procedures involving the male genital tract; and other therapeutic endocrine procedures. The relationship between abdominal paracentesis and the Arabic term “الاسْتِسْقَاء” (ascites) highlights its clinical role in managing fluid accumulation. Understanding how Nephrotoxic acute renal failure is classified as a Substance and how abdominal paracentesis fits within the broader procedural taxonomy aids clinicians in navigating regulatory terminology and selecting appropriate interventions for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum serves as the anatomical finding site for فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) and is also the location implicated in اضطراب صِفَاقِيّ (serosal disorder), indicating that peritoneal involvement is central to both the presentation of abdominal hernias and the development of serosal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: its systemic relevance. Overall, the knowledge base illustrates how abdominal and peritoneal surgical interventions, renal endoscopic and lithotripsy procedures, associated diseases, and a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic imaging techniques are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 960
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrotoxic acute renal failure is recognized as a condition that can result from exposure to certain substances that act as causative agents. These substances are directly involved in a variety of medical interventions, including abdominal paracentesis, incision and excision of the central nervous system (CNS), injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints and soft tissue, as well as other non‑operative therapeutic procedures involving the male genital tract and endocrine therapeutic procedures. The direct use of such substances during these procedures creates a potential risk for nephrotoxic injury and subsequent acute renal failure. Abdominal paracentesis, a procedure performed to remove excess fluid from the abdominal cavity, is specifically linked to the Arabic term “الاستِسْقَاء,” reflecting its role in managing ascites. Incision and excision of the CNS are associated with the skull (referred to as “lebka”) and are categorized under other non‑operative or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures. Understanding the relationship between nephrotoxic substances and these diverse procedural contexts is essential for preventing renal complications and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 847
retrieved_docs.content: Abdomen destructive procedure is the broader category that includes the specific operative technique of excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions. This procedure is performed indirectly on the peritoneum, targeting adhesions within the serosal membrane of the abdominal cavity. The peritoneum itself serves as the anatomical site where clinical findings such as فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) are identified, and it is also a location susceptible to اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ (membranous or serosal disorder). Recognizing that excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions acts on the peritoneum to resolve adhesions, while the peritoneum may also be involved in hernias and membranous disorders, provides clinicians with a comprehensive view of how therapeutic interventions, diagnostic findings, and pathological conditions are interconnected in abdominal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” includes the specific procedure referred to in Arabic as “التحويلة البطينية البريتونية” (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes an integrated clinical framework linking abdominal surgical interventions, renal replacement therapies, kidney diseases, hematologic disorders, metabolic and autoimmune conditions, and associated diagnostic and procedural practices. Abdominal destructive procedures, including excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions, are performed for acute abdomen or palpable abdominal mass and involve the peritoneal surface, which may also present with abdominal hernia or intestinal obstruction. Renal replacement access is created by external arteriovenous shunts for hemodialysis, a modality of dialysis used in chronic kidney disease (CKD), chronic renal failure, end‑stage renal disease (ESRD), and acute renal failure, and is associated with hypertension (chronic, secondary), microcytic anemia, and other blood disorders. Complications of hemodialysis include acute dialysis disorder, first‑use syndrome, dialysis‑dysfunction (dialysis disequilibrium) syndrome, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: Excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions is a surgical procedure that is performed indirectly on the peritoneum to release adhesions, and it is classified as one of the العمليات الجراحية في البطن (abdominal surgeries). The category العمليات الجراحية في البطن also includes العمليات الجراحية لقناة فالوب (Fallopian tube surgeries), indicating that surgeries of the Fallopian tube are a subset of abdominal operations. The peritoneum serves as the anatomical site where فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) is identified and is involved in اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ (peritoneal disorder). Clinical observations show that العمليات الجراحية في البطن often co‑occur with الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ (peritonitis), underscoring the close link between operative intervention in the abdomen and inflammation of the peritoneal lining. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in surgical planning, accurate diagnosis of hernias, management of peritoneal disorders, and prevention or early treatment of postoperative peritonitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: The fallopian tubes (Czech: vejcovody; Arabic: فَغْرُ البُوْق) are classified as an organ that possesses an internal cavity, making them a specific type of “organ with organ cavity.” They are integral components of the pelvic viscera, as the pelvic viscus includes the fallopian tubes among its members. In clinical coding and documentation, both cytology and transplantation are recognized as permissible qualifiers for the fallopian tubes, allowing these procedures or assessments to be specifically attributed to this structure. Inflammatory diseases affecting the female pelvic organs frequently have the fallopian tubes as a primary site of involvement, underscoring their relevance in the diagnosis and management of pelvic inflammatory conditions. Surgical interventions that target adnexal structures, such as laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures, may optionally involve the fallopian tubes, and laparoscopic lysis of adhesions is explicitly performed at the fallopian tube site. Together, these relationships highlight the anatomical classification, diagnostic considerations, and procedural contexts in which the fallopian tubes are central to female reproductive health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 406
retrieved_docs.content: The abdomen destructive procedure is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the abdomen (břicho) and is classified as a subtype of excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions. This procedure therefore has a parent relationship with excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions, indicating that it shares the same therapeutic goals of removing tissue and separating adhesions within the peritoneal cavity. While the procedure directly targets the abdominal region, it also involves the peritoneum indirectly, reflecting the anatomical continuity between the abdominal wall (břicho) and the peritoneal lining. The term břicho (the abdomen) encompasses several related concepts. It includes the sub‑entity بَطْنٌ حادّ, representing a specific abdominal condition, and is associated with a palpable mass in the abdomen (كتلة في البَطْن). Diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum is a key imaging relationship for evaluating břicho, providing clinicians with detailed visualization of abdominal structures and potential pathologies. The peritoneum, as the serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity, is an indirect procedural site for excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions. Pathological findings on the peritoneum include abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), indicating that herniation can be identified at the peritoneal level. Additionally, the peritoneum is linked to intestinal disturbance (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ), underscoring its role in gastrointestinal functional abnormalities. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how abdominal surgical procedures, imaging modalities, and peritoneal pathology are connected in the assessment and management of abdominal disorders, emphasizing the importance of a comprehensive approach that considers both direct and indirect anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 456
retrieved_docs.content: apply to arthrocentesis, diagnostic spinal tap, peritoneal dialysis, and to the equipment qualifier; the equipment itself is further associated with general anesthesia (التخدير العام), reflecting the need for appropriate devices to support safe anesthetic delivery. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured network in which MRI and ultrasound are central imaging modalities connected to anatomical, biomedical imaging, and procedural frameworks, while broader diagnostic categories such as Heat Map, emission computed tomography, and non‑imaging nuclear medicine probes or assays extend the scope of radiologic, nuclear‑medicine, and therapeutic practice, all supported by specific equipment qualifiers and radionuclide tracers for comprehensive patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: and lysis of peritoneal adhesions are indirect interventions on the peritoneum, a site of findings such as “فتق البطن” (abdominal hernia) and “اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ” (biliary disorder). Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected roles of abdominal and retroperitoneal anatomy, liver pathology, toxic substance exposure, detoxification programs, and related diagnostic and therapeutic procedures in patient care.
